coredata.dc:title: Conductive hydrogels incorporating carbon nanoparticles: A review of synthesis, performance and applications

coredata.dc:description: As one of the most rapidly expanding materials, hydrogels have gained increasing attention in a variety of fields due to their biocompatibility, degradability and hydrophilic properties, as well as their remarkable adhesion and stretchability to adapt to different surfaces. Hydrogels combined with carbon-based materials possess enhanced properties and new functionalities, in particular, conductive hydrogels have become a new area of research in the field of materials science. This review aims to provide a comprehensive overview and up-to-date examination of recent developments in the synthesis, properties and applications of conductive hydrogels incorporating several typical carbon nanoparticles such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots and carbon nanofibers. We summarize key techniques and mechanisms for synthesizing various composite hydrogels with exceptional properties, and represented applications such as wearable sensors, temperature sensors, supercapacitors and human-computer interaction reported recently. The mechanical, electrical and sensing properties of carbon nanoparticles conductive hydrogels are thoroughly analyzed to disclose the role of carbon nanoparticles in these hydrogels and key factors in the microstructure. Finally, future development of conductive hydrogels based on carbon nanoparticles is discussed including the challenges and possible solutions in terms of microstructure optimization, mechanical and other properties, and promising applications in wearable electronics and multifunctional materials.

head.ce:title: Conductive hydrogels incorporating carbon nanoparticles: A review of synthesis, performance and applications

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one of the most rapidly expanding materials, hydrogels have gained increasing attention in a variety of fields due to their biocompatibility, degradability and hydrophilic properties, as well as their remarkable adhesion and stretchability to adapt to different surfaces. Hydrogels combined with carbon-based materials possess enhanced properties and new functionalities, in particular, conductive hydrogels have become a new area of research in the field of materials science. This review aims to provide a comprehensive overview and up-to-date examination of recent developments in the synthesis, properties and applications of conductive hydrogels incorporating several typical carbon nanoparticles such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, carbon dots and carbon nanofibers. We summarize key techniques and mechanisms for synthesizing various composite hydrogels with exceptional properties, and represented applications such as wearable sensors, temperature sensors, supercapacitors and human-computer interaction reported recently. The mechanical, electrical and sensing properties of carbon nanoparticles conductive hydrogels are thoroughly analyzed to disclose the role of carbon nanoparticles in these hydrogels and key factors in the microstructure. Finally, future development of conductive hydrogels based on carbon nanoparticles is discussed including the challenges and possible solutions in terms of microstructure optimization, mechanical and other properties, and promising applications in wearable electronics and multifunctional materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, the development of flexible electronic devices has attracted much attention. Flexible electronic devices are widely used in wearable sensing (Cai et al., 2017; Hou et al., 2020; You et al., 2016), energy storage (Ahirrao et al., 2021; Liu, Zhang, et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2022) and human-computer interaction (Sun et al., 2021; Y. Wang et al., 2020). Compared with traditional electronic devices, flexible electronic devices are light, deformable, with high elasticity and ductility. The main structure of these flexible electronic devices consists of two parts: flexible substrate and conductive skeleton. Flexible polymeric substrates (such as polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS), polyurethane (PU), polyaniline (PANI), polyethylene terephthalate (PET), polyetherimide (PEI), etc.) provide flexibility and large strain deformation, and conductive skeletons (such as metal, carbon nanoparticles, MXene, etc.) provide the conductivity and sensitivity for electronic devices. Formation of conductive skeletons can be roughly divided into two general methods: (1) mixing conductive materials into the flexible substrate, and (2) covering conductive materials on the surface of the flexible substrate.

During the operation of these flexible electronic devices, external environmental stimuli (such as stretching (Shu et al., 2021; C. Wang et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2019), compression (Chen et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2022), temperature (Liu et al., 2019; Song et al., 2022), humidity (Nitta et al., 2022; Zhao et al., 2022), etc.) change the shape or conductive pathway of the flexible device. For example, the resistance value of piezoresistive electronic device will change during pressure change. With the change in ambient temperature, the resistance value of thermal-sensitive materials in temperature-responsive electronic devices also will change. Due to controllable change in related properties upon stimuli, flexible electronics devices can be applied in various aspects of different applications, however, there are still several important limitations existing. Firstly, preparation of electronic products by filling conductive materials requires a tedious process, including patterning, coating and packaging to achieve wear resistance. Secondly, due to the issue of interface incompatibility, the slippage phenomenon between the conductive material and the organic substrate will result in a delay and fluctuation in signal acquirement. Finally, because of the disadvantages of poor biocompatibility, non-degradability, and easy destruction of electronic products made from organic materials, these flexible electronic products are generally not compatible with human tissue and have very limited applications. As a result, it has become crucial to develop a flexible material with good biocompatibility, variable mechanical properties, and high stretchability for applications such as wearable sensing.

Hydrogels are 3D crosslinked network structures formed from natural or synthetic materials (chitosan (CS), agar, sodium alginate (SA), polypyrrole (PPy), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), etc.) Due to the presence of abundant hydrophilic groups in the crosslinked network of hydrogels, they can accommodate a large amount of water. The crosslinking network of hydrogels is composed of chemical and physical bonds, and the combination of these two types of crosslinking leads to the formation of the hydrogel network. Liu et al. prepared multifunctional double-network hydrogels by combining the host-guest interaction of β-cyclodextrin and ferrocene with the dynamic borate ester bond of polyvinyl alcohol and borax. Due to the dynamic host-guest interaction and the presence of the borate ester bond, the resulting DN hydrogels exhibit excellent stretching, self-healing, and remodeling properties (Liu, Ren, et al., 2021). Most of the polymer chains of hydrogels are reliably biocompatible, so they have been extensively studied in biomedical applications such as cell culture (Ozawa et al., 2016; Smith et al., 2021), wound dressings (Rajati et al., 2022; Y. Wang et al., 2022), tissue engineering (Wang & Feng, 2022; Zhang et al., 2019), etc. In addition, applications of hydrogels in sensors (Fan et al., 2022; Ling et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2020; Wu, Yang, & Wu, 2021), electronic skin (Chen, Qian, et al., 2022; Yi et al., 2021), human motion detection (Ling et al., 2021, 2023; Song et al., 2023) and coatings (Bai et al., 2022; Peng et al., 2020) also show great potential due to large strain properties. The advantages of hydrogels in these applications are as follows: (1) Hydrogels have excellent stretching properties, allowing for a wide range of detection during testing. Some hydrogels which have strains of up to several thousand, can maintain good stability under large strains with little energy dissipation during stretching. (2) Hydrogels also have excellent viscoelasticity, and the presence of hydrogen bonds enables conformal deposition to different substrates, providing a variety of options for applications, such as actuation micromachines and human body interaction. (3) Hydrogels are 3D crosslinked network structures that are easily combined with some antifreeze materials, giving them a wide working temperature range to meet the demands of harsh conditions. For example, glycerin can enhance the antifreeze performance of hydrogels. Despite of those advantages, in practical use of electronic products made from hydrogels, it can be observed that the conductivity of hydrogels greatly affects their work efficiency and accuracy. The limited electrical conductivity in combination with excellent stretchability of most current hydrogels restricts their use in hydrogel-based flexible electronics. To address this issue, research and development of highly conductive and multifunctional hydrogels are necessary. Generally, conducting hydrogels can be classified into three categories: (1) metal conductive hydrogels, (2) conductive polymer hydrogels, and (3) carbon nanoparticles conductive hydrogels (CNPCH). The advantages and disadvantages of different types of hydrogels can be clearly seen in Table 1 .

Carbon nanoparticles (such as carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene, carbon nanofibers (CNFs) and carbon dots (CDs) etc.) have excellent mechanical properties, electrical properties and large specific surface area, and can serve as ideal nanoscale fillers for constructing conductive and reinforced hydrogels. On one hand, carbon fillers can improve the mechanical properties of hydrogels. The addition of carbon nanoparticles could form crosslinking junctions with polymer molecular chains and improve the tensile stress of hydrogels. Also, carbon nanoparticles enhance the toughness of hydrogel and enable self-recovery ability of hydrogels. This is mainly due to the reversible hydrogen bonds formed between carbon nanoparticles and polymer chains. When the strain on the hydrogel is removed, the crosslinked network elastically contracts and the hydrogen bonds recombine, leading to the self-recovery performance. On the other hand, compared to traditional fillers, carbon nanoparticles with electrical properties and large specific surface area result in a low percolation threshold in the hydrogel at which a highly conductive network would form by interconnection of distributed carbon fillers. In addition, carbon nanoparticles are easily modified by strong acids to carry functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) which are favorable in the formation of robust and conductive crosslinked network during the hydrogel synthesis process.

With the development of hydrogels, there have been a number of review articles on the synthesis mechanism of conductive hydrogels (Zhu, Ni, et al., 2022) and the filling mechanism of carbon nanoparticles (Mittal et al., 2015), and in recent years, on applications of in the fields of energy (Anjali et al., 2019; Bashir et al., 2021), antibacterial function (Fatima et al., 2021), waste water treatment (Raza et al., 2022; Zeng et al., 2017), tissue engineering (Asadi et al., 2018; Ghovvati et al., 2022), and drug delivery (Vashist et al., 2018). In this review, in conjunction with previously reported experiments, we summarize the advantages of carbon nanoparticles in developing high performance conductive hydrogels. Significant alteration in mechanical and electrical properties resulting from the integration of carbon nanoparticles is analyzed, and the underlying mechanisms behind the enhanced performance of conductive hydrogels are summarized. Then, applications of CNPCH in wearable sensing, temperature sensing, human-computer interaction and supercapacitors are summarized and analyzed. Finally, potential use of carbon nanoparticles in hydrogels are analyzed and forecasted. As shown in Fig. 1 , CNPCH are divided into three categories in this paper: (1) CNT conductive hydrogels, (2) graphene conductive hydrogels, (3) other carbon-material (e.g., CNFs and CDs) conductive hydrogels. Next, we will review these several types of conductive hydrogels in detail and their applications in various fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and mechanism analysis of CNPCH
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon can be structured in many different ways, including CNTs, graphene, CNFs and CDs, with widely tunable properties. In these carbon nanoparticles, high conductivity, excellent mechanical properties and large specific surface area provide many opportunities to develop next-generation functional hydrogels (Li et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2017, 2021). Currently, we are facing great challenge to effectively incorporate carbon nanoparticles to form conductive hydrogels that possess sufficient robustness, high elasticity and conductivity to meet the required specifications. Through Table 2 , we summarized the advantages and disadvantages of carbon nanoparticles with different structures, which has a very important reference significance for the selection of carbon nanoparticles to form hydrogels.

Since the discovery of CNTs in 1991, mass production of CNTs is produced via various methods such as arc evaporation, laser ablation, chemical vapor deposition (CVD), electrolysis, flame synthesis etc. Currently, the most common way to prepare CNTs is CVD, in which the CNT length, diameter, alignment, purity, density, and orientation can be well controlled. There are several advantages of CNTs in the development of flexible conductive sensors: (1) Their 1D structure with high aspect ratio brings exceptional flexibility. More attractively, this excellent structure allows CNTs to be bent, twisted, and knotted with minimal damage to the material. When the forces are removed, CNTs can restore to their original state. (2) Single CNT have high tensile strength due to the presence of sp2 bonds between each carbon atom. It takes a significant amount of energy to break sp2 bonds, making them theoretically stronger than sp3 bonds in diamonds. (3) Individual CNTs not only have a large specific surface area, but also can be grafted by oxygen-containing groups through strong acid treatment. These characteristics enable CNTs to combine with more materials, achieve multi-material composites, and provide excellent conductive networks in composites (Chen, Yang, et al., 2022; Dong et al., 2013).

The dispersion of CNTs largely determines the properties of the composites. A common way to improve the dispersion of CNTs is by forming covalent or non-covalent bonds. Lu et al. (Lu et al., 2021) enhanced the distribution of CNTs within the hydrogel matrix by adding 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidin-1-yloxy (TEMPO) oxidized cellulose nanofibers (TOCNs), because TOCNs can disperse CNTs by forming CH-π linkages with the π-conjugated system of CNTs through hydrophobic surfaces containing a large number of –CH groups. The close hydrophobic interaction between CNTs and the hydrophobic surfaces of TOCNs, also the electrostatic repulsion between the carboxylate groups of TOCNs at a longer distance together maintained good dispersion of CNTs in hydrogel. The tensile strength (∼0.15 MPa), tensile modulus (∼84.18 kPa), and toughness (∼1.95 MJ/m3) of TOCN-2.0% CNT/PAAM were 7.5, 4.9 and 9.7 times higher than those of pure PAAM, respectively (Fig. 2 (a)). Jiao et al. (Jiao et al., 2021) developed a crosslinked chemically modified cellulose nanofiber-CNT/polyacrylic acid (TOCNF-CNT/PAA) hydrogel. The introduction of carboxyl groups on the surface of TOCNFs after TEMPO oxidation treatment resulted in electrostatic repulsion among TOCNFs, thus promoting uniform and stable dispersion of TOCNFs. The dispersed TOCNFs adhere to the surface of the CNTs and disperse the CNTs through electrostatic interaction. In addition, the carboxyl groups present on the surface of TOCNFs can form hydrogen bonds with PAA polymer chains, which further enhances the mechanical properties of hydrogels. Therefore, the synergistic effect of PAA, TOCNFs, and CNTs leads to excellent mechanical properties in the hydrogel. The CNTs content increased from 0.3 wt% to 0.7 wt%, and the tensile strength of the TOCNF-CNT/PAA hydrogel increased from 37.9 kPa to 59.16 kPa. Ren et al. (Ren et al., 2021) synthesized a water-soluble crosslinking agent PCD-CHO. Based on O-carboxymethyl chitosan (O-CMCS) and PVA, they achieved this by using Schiff base reaction between PCD-CHO and O-CMCS, and then crosslinked PVA by adding borax. To improve the electrical and mechanical properties of the hydrogels, OPPC hydrogels (O-CMCS, PCD-CHO, PVA, and CNTs) were synthesized by adding CNTs. A simple melt processing method was used to prepare the hydrogels. Results showed that adding 0.5 wt% CNTs significantly increased the maximum tensile strength (0.186 MPa) and strain (164%) of OPPC hydrogels compared to hydrogels without CNTs (Fig. 2(b)).

Hydrogen bonding and hydrophobic interactions between CNTs and polymer chains can also improve the mechanical properties and dispersion of hydrogels. Fan et al. (Fan, Geng, Wang, & Gu, 2022) synthesized a dual-network hydrogel GPPBC (gelatin, PCH-FC-CHO, PVA, borax, and CNTs). An aldehyde-containing supramolecular crosslinking agent (PCD-Fc-CHO) using β-cyclodextrin (β-CD) and diene iron (Fc), which was then reacted with gelatin via Schiff base reaction to form the first network of hydrogels. The second network was formed through dynamic borate ester bonds between borax and PVA. By adding CNTs to the hydrogels exhibited improved mechanical and electrical properties. CNTs led to an increase in tensile stress to 26.4 ± 1.3 kPa and tensile strain to 1156 ± 66% which can be attributed to the hydrophilic groups on the CNTs, which can form strong hydrogen bonds with gelatin and/or PVA. Sun et al. (Sun et al., 2020) oxidized multi-walled CNTs (MWCNTs) using nitric acid and mixed the resulting oxidized MWCNTs (oxCNTs) with PAAm to form conductive nanocomposite hydrogels. The carboxyl groups on the surface of oxCNTs can form hydrogen bonds with the hydroxyl groups between gelatin chains, allowing for the even dispersion of oxCNTs in the hydrogel. When the content of resoxcnts was 1 wt%, the tensile strength of PAAm-oxCNTs hydrogels was 2.55 times higher than that of pure PAAm hydrogels, reaching up to 0.71 MPa, while the fracture strain did not change. These changes can be attributed to the rich physical interactions between oxCNTs, gelatin chains, and PAAm chains, with the good dispersion of oxCNTs playing a particularly important role (Fig. 2(c)). Qin et al. (Qin et al., 2020) employed amphiphilic sodium dodecyl sulfate by incorporating hydrophobic CNTs into HAPAAM, which ensured uniform dispersion of CNTs in the hydrogel network. The tensile strength, fracture strain and toughness of the conductive hydrogel were enhanced by the tight bonding between the polyacrylamide chains and CNTs due to the nano-enhancement effect and the hydrophobicity of CNTs. The tensile mechanical properties of HAPAAM hydrogel (0.0 wt% CNTs) were poor, with a tensile stress of 0.056 MPa, a fracture strain of about 2500%, and a toughness of 0.74 MJ/m3. The tensile strength of CNT/HAPAAM hydrogel is 0.267 MPa, the strain at break is about 3000%, and the toughness is 3.42 MJ/m3 when 0.5 wt% CNTs are added. Meanwhile, CNTs can form hydrophobic interactions with the hydrogel matrix as physical crosslinking sites, and the high electrical conductivity of CNTs also gives the hydrogel a large strain sensing window and fatigue resistance sensing performances (Fig. 2(d)).

The large aspect ratio of CNTs makes it easier to load a larger number of various materials on their surface, thereby enabling the creation of multi-material conductive hydrogels. By integrating different materials into the hydrogel system, this can also provide new opportunities for the advancement of hydrogels. Cheng et al. (Cheng, Li, et al., 2021) composed a high strain stress sensor through the composite made of MWCNTs, PVA, and PAAM. The multi-wall CNTs (MWCNTs) serve as conductive channels and provide mechanical reinforcement, while the PVA chains with abundant hydrogen bonds have excellent self-adhesive ability, and the performance enhancement brought by the MWCNTs makes the use of the composite as electronic skins much more feasible (Fig. 2(e)). Liang et al. (Liang et al., 2019) combined GT-DA with polydopamine-encapsulated CNTs (CNT-PDA) to form conductive GT-DA/CS/CNT. Through oxidative coupling of catechol moieties, the mechanical strength was effectively improved by the addition of CNT-PDA. Moreover, the hydrogels incorporating higher CNT-PDA concentrations also exhibited higher compressive stresses when the CNT-PDA content was increased from 0 to 1 wt%, 2 wt%, and 4 wt% at the same strain (from 68.0 kPa at 80% strain to 78.9 kPa, 98.8 kPa and 115.9 kPa at 80% strain) (Fig. 2(f)).

In summary, the advantages brought by the addition of CNTs to the hydrogel system are as follows: (1) the dispersion of CNTs brings more cross-linking points to the hydrogel and forms a highly conductive network, (2) the oxygen-containing functional groups grafted on CNTs can improve the adhesion and network structure of the hydrogel, and (3) CNTs bring more possibilities of material compounding to the hydrogel with enhanced properties and newly expanded functions.

Graphene, as a 2D honeycomb structure with sp2 hybridization, has unique electrical, chemical, and mechanical properties (Yu et al., 2017). The research of graphene can cover various fields such as energy, medicine, and chemistry (Huang et al., 2020; Priya Swetha et al., 2018; Tsang et al., 2020). Graphene oxide (GO) and reduced graphene oxide (rGO) are mainly used as graphene conductive hydrogels. GO can be prepared by many methods such as mechanical exfoliation of graphite, epitaxial growth on SiC or metal substrates by CVD and other methods, thermal or chemical reduction of GO fabricated by exfoliation of graphite oxide, and exfoliation of graphite intercalation compounds. At present research, GO and rGO are widely used in the CNPCH. A common method for preparing GO is the Hummers process, in which the oxidation reaction first produces hydroxyl groups at the edges and defects of graphite, followed by the oxidation of the hydroxyl groups at the edges to form carbon groups. At the same time, the carbon atoms on the base are oxidized to form hydroxyl groups. In the presence of strong acids, adjacent hydroxyl groups undergo dehydration and condensation to form epoxy groups. With the process of oxidation, the amount of carboxyl group at the edge increases further until that the edges are completely oxidized and reach saturation. Then when the amount of oxidant increases to the critical value, the ketone and aldehyde group will be oxidized to carboxyl group (Razaq et al., 2022). However, the surface oxygen-containing functional groups of GO make it almost insulating, so that GO usually can be reduced to rGO with better electrical conductivity by high temperature treatment or mixing aqueous solutions of dopamine. Using the self-polymerization and reduction of dopamine, GO can be reduced to rGO (Xu et al., 2010).

The addition of GO to hydrogels leads to enhanced mechanical properties, owing to its layered structure and abundant functional groups (such as epoxy, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups) that make it compatible with polymers, colloids, and amphiphiles. As for rGO, its large specific surface area provides more charge trapping sites for the hydrogel, thereby increasing its electrical conductivity. Wang et al. (Z. Wang et al., 2022) reported a self-powered strain sensor based on GO-polyacrylamide (GO-PAM) hydrogel, which could analyze normal and abnormal human gait and had a very outstanding potential for medical applications. The tensile strength of the 0.02 wt% GO-PAM hydrogel was 197 kPa, and the maximum compressive stress reached was 0.76 MPa when the compressive strain was 80%. After 10 cycles, the hydrogel had a 92.7% and 90% recovery rate, respectively, indicating that the 0.02 wt% GO-PAM hydrogels had good durability at such high strains (Fig. 3 (a)). Zhou et al. (Zhou et al., 2021) improved the electrical conductivity of the hydrogel by introducing rGO into the hydrogel system. During the compression test, it was found that after 15 successive loading and unloading cycles, there was no interval between two cycles. The hysteresis of the first cycle was larger, the cyclic compression curves roughly overlapped with little deviation, and the permanent deformation after loading was small with good self-recovery ability. Meanwhile, the energy dissipation rate of the conductive hydrogel was as high as 65%, which was inseparable from the chelating effect of –NH2 and –COOH groups in the hydrogel hybrid system and the hydrogen bonding between the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of rGO. Thus, the conductive hydrogel had good self-recovery ability (Fig. 3(b)).

GO can also be reduced by dopamine, and dopamine-reduced rGO (PDA-rGO) is more highly chemical stable due to the removal of most oxygen-containing functional groups. When PDA-rGO is used as a conductive filler, PDA-rGO ensures that the hydrogel maintains good sensitivity under external stimuli, and the presence of abundant hydrogen bonds between PDA-rGO and other composites provide a promising approach for self-healing conductive hydrogels. Xie et al. (Xie et al., 2021, 2022) and Jing et al. (Jing et al., 2018) reduced GO by dopamine self-polymerization and introduced it into the hydrogel system as a conductive filler. The introduction of PDA-rGO after dopamine reduction led to a denser physical crosslinking point in the hydrogel network, which increased mechanical properties but led to lower tensile strain due to agglomeration. Moreover, the addition of PDA-rGO still provided a conductive pathway for the hydrogel and improved the sensitivity of the hydrogel. In addition, PDA-rGO improves the self-healing ability of the hydrogel by forming strong hydrogen and coordination bonds between PEDOT: PSS and Fe3+ (Fig. 3(c and d)). The introduction of antifreeze materials (such as glycerol) into the hydrogel containing PDA-rGO can also form a composite conductive hydrogel to change the temperature response range of the hydrogel. But it is worth noting that the addition of these materials also affects the electrical conductivity of the hydrogel to some extent. For example, the addition of glycerol affects the movement of free ions in the hydrogel, which in turn affects the electrical conductivity of the hydrogel (Fig. 3(e)).

When GO and rGO are combined, the abundance of functional groups on the GO surface provides more hydrogen bonds for the water gel, thereby improving its stretchability and adhesion. GO can also serve as a dispersion material for rGO, ensuring good dispersion of rGO which in turn contributes to the high conductivity and provides ion channels for the hydrogel, thereby enhancing its sensitivity and stability. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2021) synthesized hydrogels with honeycomb-like dense micro-networks and uniformly dispersed conductive fillers by co-doping GO and rGO. GO with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups as surfactant promoter hindered the aggregation of rGO in the organic hydrogel, resulting in excellent mechanical properties of the conductive hydrogel with a tensile strain of about 600% and a breaking strength of 3.1 MPa. The hydrogel also demonstrated excellent mechanical stability and repeatability in multiple compression cycling tests with a large hysteresis return line in the 1st cycle, which effectively revealed energy dissipation. A significant overlap could be seen in the next 2nd to 20th loadings, demonstrating its self-recovery properties and elasticity. These changes could be attributed to the combination of the two materials, which affected the van der Waals interactions with the organic polymer, caused the hydrogel to produce regular closed pores, also formed a honeycomb micro-network with aligned pore walls (Fig. 3(f)).

In summary, the advantages brought by the addition of graphene to the hydrogel system are as follows: (1) GO containing oxygen functional groups reduce the interfacial forces, making GO possess hydrophilicity and better dispersion in the hydrogel network (2) rGO is stable and conductive, which provides more conductive pathways and enhances the electrical stability of the hydrogel (3) the larger lamellar structure endows outstanding advantages in multi-material composite.

In addition to CNTs and graphene, other carbon nanoparticles have very promising applications in conductive hydrogels. CDs, as 0D materials, have a smaller microscopic size and are easily soluble in water, exhibiting strong chemical inertness, better biocompatibility and lower biotoxicity (Wareing et al., 2021). CNFs are inorganic polymer fibers, containing more than 90% carbon. CNFs inherit some of the advantages of CNTs, including good electrical conductivity, mechanical strength and easy modification, but in terms of preparation, CNFs are simpler and more efficient.

CDs have several advantages, including their small size and water solubility, as well as the presence of many oxygen-containing groups on their surface, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups. By incorporating CDs into the 3D structure of the hydrogel, the hydrogel system can become denser, thereby enhancing its mechanical properties. Hu et al. (Hu et al., 2017) proposed tough polyacrylamide-co-acrylic acid CDs-iron ion (PAmAc-Cdot-Fe3+) nano conductive hydrogels with a double cross-linked network. CDs can bond with iron ions in hydrogels, forming strong coordination bonds with the carboxyl groups on polymer chains. The mechanical properties of hydrogels are greatly improved by the combination of CDs, Fe3+, and PAmAc. When the CDs content increased from 0.25 wt% to 2 wt%, the tensile stress and Young's modulus were gradually increased from 3.63 MPa to 0.55 MPa–4.23 MPa and 1.35 MPa respectively. This is due to the increase in the number of physical cross-linking points among PAmAc polymer chains and enhanced hydrogen bonding interactions as the CDs content increases, resulting in an increase in cross-link density and producing a more robust network structure (Fig. 4 (a)). Arnab Shit et al. (Shit et al., 2022) fabricated a type of CDs (cPDA/TiO2@f-cPDA/TiO2)loaded conductive hydrogel, where TiO2-immo-bilized carbonized polydopamine-perfluorosilica (f-cPDA/TiO2) and carbonized polydopamine (cPDA/TiO2) are utilized. Under light, the release of cPDA from large-sized f-cPDA/TiO2 and cPDA/TiO2 induces a hydrophilic-hydrophobic transition inside the CD hydrogel network, forming hydrogen bonds. The results can be found that the maximum tensile strength of the CD-hydrogel without light treatment is 0.27 MPa, the elongation at break is 212%, and the Young's modulus is 24.23 kPa, while the tensile stress of the CD-hydrogel after 9 h of visible light treatment was 0.83 MPa, the elongation at break was 531%, and the Young's modulus was 84.87 kPa. The experimental results showed that the visible light treatment simultaneously improved the mechanical strength including tensile of the CD hydrogel, and the generation of hydrogen bonds promoted carrier migration and enhanced the electrical conductivity of the hydrogel (Fig. 4(b)).

CNFs, with high Young's modulus and tensile strength, play an important role in enhancing the mechanical properties of various composite materials. CNFs also have the advantages of good thermal stability and large specific surface area. The advantages of CNFs perfectly fit the required functions for conductive hydrogel fillers, and CNFs are simple to prepare and easy to produce, which also has a considerable reference value for future hydrogel industrial applications. Cheng et al. (Cheng et al., 2020) prepared polyvinyl alcohol/carbon nanofiber powder (PVA/CFP)hydrogels by the freeze-thaw cycling method. The addition of CFPs brought about a loss of hydrogel strain, but its dispersion in the hydrogel resulted in high stress and electrical conductivity. The Young's modulus of pure PVA, PVA/CFP0.2, PVA/CFP0.6, and PVA/CFP1.0 hydrogels were calculated to be 195.11, 221.51, 251.06, and 279.19 kPa, respectively, showing an upward tendency, which was attributed to the mechanical reinforcement of CFP. Simultaneously, the measured conductivity of pure PVA, PVA/CFP0.2, PVA/CFP0.6, and PVA/CFP1.0 hydrogels were 12.0, 15.4, 17.1, and18.9 mS/m in turn, indicating the addition of CFP enhanced the conductivity of the hydrogel (Fig. 4(c)). Pan et al. (Pan et al., 2020) synthesized a double-network polymer hydrogel combining CNFs with a double-network polymer consisting of a physical cross-linked agar network and a covalent cross-linked polyacrylamide (PAAM) network. The introduction of CNFs significantly improved the piezoresistive sensitivity of the hydrogel strain sensor through the electron tunneling effect, and also improved the mechanical properties (strength and elastic modulus) of the hydrogel. However, in the absence of CNFs addition, the hydrogels had high tensile properties but poor tensile strength and fracture strain. When the CNFs content increased from 0.25 wt% to 1 wt%, the corresponding tensile strength increased from 373 kPa to 689 kPa and the fracture strain increased from 1151% to 1456%, respectively. Similarly, the elastic modulus and absorbed energy of DN hydrogels with the addition of 1 wt% CNFs increased by more than 2.5 and 1.5 times respectively when compared with the unmodified DN hydrogels. The incorporation of CNFs with PVP in water was resulted in the formation of a physical network between CNFs and the polymer matrix, where CNFs acted as additional dynamic cross-linking sites and more cross-linking sites added to the strong hydrogel network (Fig. 4(d)).

In summary, the advantages brought by the addition of CDs and CNFs to the hydrogel system are as follows: (1) CDs are easy to prepare and chemically stable, and it is easy to be doped into the network structure of hydrogels due to the existence of surface oxygen-containing functional groups, and various functional groups can meet the mechanical changes of hydrogels. (2) CNFs provide more crosslinking points and can significantly reduce costs compared with other fillers, overcoming some of the drawbacks of hydrogels themselves. At the same time, CNFs are more likely to bring the development of hydrogels out of the laboratory and improve the practicality of hydrogels.

The dispersion of carbon nanoparticles plays a significant role in determining the properties of composites (Alam et al., 2018). In particular, different types of carbon nanoparticles exhibit varying compatibility with polymer skeleton due to their dispersion. CDs, being 0D materials with a small size, can form an aqueous solution through the hydrothermal method and can be easily surface-modified and dispersed in polymer skeleton, thereby facilitating their combination with the polymer skeleton. The degree of oxidation of graphene determines its dispersion in the polymer skeleton. Due to the high concentration of oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of GO, good polymer combinations can be formed, establishing hydrogen bonding. However, the compatibility of GO with different polymers varies. For example, the hydroxyl group of GO forms a hydrogen bond with PVA, which acts as both a hydrogen bond acceptor and donor. In contrast, for PEG and GO, PEG acts as a hydrogen bond receptor, indicating that the compatibility of GO and PVA is superior to PEG (Suter et al., 2020). Due to their hydrophobicity and other properties, CNTs and CNFs are not easily dispersed and exhibit poor compatibility with polymer skeleton. Chemical methods to improve dispersion and compatibility include surface chemical modifications and surface modifiers. Surface chemical modification involves grafting hydroxyl, carboxyl, and other oxygen-containing groups to the surface via strong acids and then covalently bonding the polymer to form a dispersion. The principle of surface modifier is to use surfactants to disperse carbon nanoparticles and polymers through electrostatic repulsion and van der Waals forces of non-covalent connection. Ultrasonic-assisted and melt-processing methods are also used to disperse carbon nanoparticles. Ultrasonic-assisted processing involves subjecting carbon nanoparticles in the precursor solution to intense vibrations using ultrasonic waves to disperse them. Melt processing involves melting a thermoplastic polymer into a viscous liquid and dispersing carbon nanoparticles by shearing mixing.

CNPCH boasts excellent mechanical properties, and the even dispersion of carbon nanoparticles further enhances its performance by providing a larger number of soft contact points within the hydrogel due to the smaller osmotic threshold of the nanoparticles. Additionally, the carbon nanoparticles' large specific surface area and electrical conductivity significantly improve CNPCH's conductivity. In CNPCH, the hydrogel undergoes force deformation that disrupts the conductive pathway connecting the carbon nanoparticles. At certain tensile thresholds, the hydrogel's resistance value experiences a significant change, resulting in a sudden increase in sensitivity. Table 3 summarizes CNPCH's sensing mode, operating range, conductivity, and sensitivity, which are essential considerations for its application. These data can provide valuable guidance for the effective use of CNPCH. Overall, CNPCH demonstrates significant potential as a highly sensitive and versatile hydrogel for a broad range of applications.

The rapid development of hydrogels has aroused tremendous interest of researchers in recent years, which facilitates the exploration of hydrogels' various advantages in tissue regeneration (Bai et al., 2018), drug delivery (Lynch et al., 2020), biosensing (Herrmann et al., 2021), medical imaging (Dong et al., 2021), brakes (Cheng, Chen, & Li, 2021), wastewater treatment (Van Tran et al., 2018), and food detection (Lin et al., 2023). In biomedical applications, the biocompatibility of hydrogels enables the delivery of targeted drugs in the human body, and the 3D network structure of hydrogels promotes drug delivery process. In sensing and detection applications, the hydrogel's structure can be crosslinked via many conductive and detective materials to enhance its conductivity and sensitivity. Furthermore, CNPCH have promising applications in wearable sensing, temperature sensing, supercapacitors, and human-computer interaction. The benefits of CNPCH are highlighted by analyzing their changing performance.

Since PDMS, PU, PEI and other flexible substrates are widely compounded with conductive materials to construct flexible sensors, there has been rapid development of flexible wearable sensors in medicine, security and communication. In terms of biomonitoring sensors such as heart rate and respiratory rate, measured information can be uploaded to the cloud through real-time monitoring of wearable sensors, through where we can easily find the market potential of these wearable devices. However, the wearable devices formed by ordinary flexible bases have low stretchability (<200%). In recent years, hydrogels can be used instead of these polymeric flexible bases for wearable sensing applications due to their inherent high stretchability (4000%) (Lin et al., 2019). Due to their unique structure and properties, carbon nanoparticles can provide more conductive sites in the 3D network of hydrogels, improving the migration rate of carriers and the sensitivity of sensors. Additionally, the functional groups of carbon nanoparticles offer more possibilities for wearable sensors, such as monitoring different conditions and using different polymer substrates. Moreover, carbon nanoparticles have good biocompatibility, enabling CNPCH to better interact with the human body, making it suitable for wearable sensing applications.

The biocompatibility of hydrogel makes them more conducive for wearable sensing applications, as they can be directly in contact with the human body. In addition, the fatigue resistance and self-healing properties allow the hydrogel sensors to more accurately identify and analyze changes in the signal and periodically monitor usage. In terms of conductive composite material, hydrogels are inherent 3D structures entangled by polymer chains, which makes it easier to combine with carbon nanoparticles to improve the sensing sensitivity. CNPCH can uniformly contact to the surface of human body. Human movement will cause strain variation of hydrogels, changing the resistance value of hydrogels and reaching the monitoring target of movement. It is well known that when the human joints are bent at different angles, the hydrogel shows different sensing performance, which makes it easier to analyze the human behaviors. Not only these large movements can cause the sensing of hydrogel, but also the subtle changes of human pulse can be observed by the CNPCH (Fig. 5 (a and b)) (Deng et al., 2021; Wu, Fan, et al., 2021).

In addition to the contact with the human body, the adhesion property of hydrogels to different materials also plays a very important role. The hydrogels can adhere to the prosthesis to monitor the movement of the prosthesis, so wearable sensing applications of CNPCH show a trend of increasing especially in some industrial applications as well (Fig. 5(c)) (Jing et al., 2018). An array of CNPCH is shown below, which can be pressed to display different letters, suggesting that they can be used as wearable sensors to sense external stimuli (Fig. 5(d)) (Yu et al., 2022). What's more, the structural changes of the CNPCH also make it possible to detect more substances such as moisture and sweat. The unique layered structure of the CNPCH makes it more significantly moisture absorbent, and based on this, an exercise headband is designed to monitor the health level of the human body by monitoring the absorption of sweat (Fig. 5(e)) (Zhao, Zhang, et al., 2021).

As a daily monitoring method, temperature sensor can assess whether the growing conditions of vegetables are suitable by monitoring the temperature inside vegetable greenhouses. Air conditioning can monitor temperature at any time, through automatic temperature sensing and adjustment; Ovens can bake various types of bread based on temperature, among other factors. Due to its high-water-content and 3D structure, hydrogels can be enhanced to be sensitive to temperature by monitoring water content at different temperatures and introducing thermal response materials (such as Pt, Cu, VO2, etc.). Due to their large specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity, CNTs and graphene have a good response to temperature changes and could enhance the stability and sensitivity of the sensor for temperature monitoring.

The large aspect ratio and excellent electrical and thermal properties of CNTs in conductive hydrogels allow for the reflection of temperature change through monitoring the change in electrical signals. An et al. (An et al., 2020)introduced MWCNTs into composite polyacrylamide/Fe3+-polyacrylic acid (PAM/Fe3+-PAA) double network (DN) hydrogels, which can be used as a novel temperature sensor channel material due to the excellent thermal conductivity of MWCNTs that improve the temperature sensitivity of DN hydrogels. The thermal sensitivity increased from 2.2 to 9.4% per degree Celsius as the tensile strain of the hydrogel increased from 0% to 200%. The tensile stress applied on the stretchable hydrogels to produce a large tensile deformation, which increased the contact area of the hydrogel to the heat source and enhanced the thermal response. Also, the reduction in thickness shortens the pathway in the stretched state, accelerating the heat transfer response. The sensing model was also made based on the temperature sensing properties by wrapping it around the knee joint (Fig. 6 (a)). Zhu et al. (Zhu, Xu, et al., 2022) also used CNT conductive hydrogel as a wearable bracelet to monitor thermal response in real time through the incorporation of CNTs. They also equipped the sensor with a high-temperature protection component in series with an alarm circuit and a liquid crystal display (LCD), which will display “Hot” to avoid sensor damage when the temperature is too high (Fig. 6(b)).

The graphene conductive hydrogels also have the function of temperature sensing due to the thermal conductivity of graphene itself. The relative resistance change of the hydrogel sensor designed by Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2021)decreases with increasing temperature, and the slope of the current-voltage curve increases with temperature raising. Furthermore, the temperature of the volunteer can be monitored by the thermal property of the hydrogel, so as to obtain the health status of the volunteer (Fig. 6(c)). Xie et al. (Xie et al., 2021) also explored the thermal properties of graphene conductive hydrogels and found that the resistance decreased sharply due to the raising in temperature during heating, while it increased during cooling (Fig. 6(d)).

Stretchable supercapacitors must maintain their electrical properties while undergoing large strains due to the growing requirement of wearable and portable electronic devices. Although the energy dissipation is inevitable during bending or stretching, the self-healing property of hydrogels can effectively mitigate the energy loss. Importantly, 3D porous conductive hydrogels serve as a useful reservoir layer for electrolyte and electrode, with potential applications in advanced energy storage systems such as supercapacitors. It is easily processed, highly hydrophilic and highly stretchable, which are suitable characteristics for creating flexible stretchable capacitors. The large specific surface area and conductivity of carbon nanoparticles, when dispersed evenly in hydrogels, can increase the contact area with electrolytes, provide abundant active sites, shorten the conductive path, and improve the rate of ion transport. Additionally, the surface-grafted functional groups of carbon nanoparticles can enhance the hygroscopic ability of hydrogels, accelerate the transfer of charge, and improve their storage capacity. Conductive hydrogels made of carbon nanoparticles enhance the capacity, reduce internal resistance, and extend the cycle life of supercapacitors. This serves as a valuable reference for further development of flexible supercapacitors (Zou et al., 2021).

CNTs have the potential in reducing internal resistance and improving cycling stability in supercapacitors. Chen et al. (Chen et al., 2019) prepared supercapacitors by using gold nanoparticle/CNT/polypyrrole (GCP@PPy) hydrogel electrodes, with CNT-free GCP hydrogel as the electrolyte. The capacitors exhibited highly structural integrity and stable conductivity under tensile deformation, and the specific capacitance did not decay when the bending angle changed from 0° to 180°, both of which indicated that the devices maintained mechanical robustness and structural stability during bending (Fig. 7 (a)). Ran et al. (Ren et al., 2022) prepared PPy/carbon all-solid-state supercapacitor (PCSCs) hydrogels based on layered PPy/CNT electrodes and highly stretchable dual-network polymer hydrogel electrolytes with LiCl/ethylene glycol as the mixed solvent. In the results, it can be seen that the PCSCs exhibit excellent mechanical stability in the bending test. The capacitance retention was close to 100% after multiple bending. In particular, the pre-stretched PPy/CNT film electrode allows the whole device to be stretched while maintaining stable electrochemical activity, and during the stretching process, the folds of the PPy/CNT film gradually flatten out, allowing the electrode to be in full contact with the electrolyte and promoting enhanced electrochemical performance (Fig. 7(b)).

Graphene can provide abundant active surfaces and short ion diffusion channels in supercapacitors due to its 2D lamellar structure. Hybrid hydrogels of bio-based chitosan with porous GO were prepared by Jiang et al. (Jiang et al., 2022) who use microwave-assisted hydrothermal method. The prepared hydrogels have good electrical conductivity, good internal cross-linking, and good dispersion of the layered structure, and can be used as additive-free electrodes without any additives and binders. The highest value of MCHG is 432 F/g at 0.5 A/g according to the capacitance calculated from the GCD curve, which is closely related to the functionalization of the material surface and the more developed layered porous structure. Moreover, the micropores of the hydrogels directly contribute to the energy storage capacity. On the other hand, the in-plane nanopores of GO shorten the ion diffusion path, and ions can diffuse directly through the in-plane rather than laterally through the graphene sheets (Fig. 7(c)). Nickel ferrite nanoparticles (NiFe2O4) prepared by Sardana et al. (Sardana et al., 2022) were dispersed onto reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (NFG) to form NiFe2O4/NFG nanocomposites. Then the polyaniline hydrogel was polymerized on the NFG nanosheets to prepare ternary polyaniline/NiFe2O4/rGO hydrogel (PNFG) nanocomposites on carbon cloth, which can be further used as binder-free supercapacitor electrodes. The capacitor still has a large discharge time and a very small voltage drop even at 10 A/g, indicating that the electrode material has a low internal resistance. Moreover, the GCD curves of the synthetic electrodes are almost similar in shape, indicating that the electrodes have significant rate capability. Also, after 5000 consecutive cycles at 10 A/g, the supercapacitor can still maintain 83.53% of the original capacitance value, which has good cycling performance (Fig. 7(d)). Shen et al. (Shen et al., 2023) developed a method to prepare nanocomposite hydrogels (PAAm/CS-EDTA/GO) by mixing ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA) modified chitosan (CS) with GO in acrylamide (AAm) crosslinking polymerization. They constructed a sandwich supercapacitor via the PAAm/CS-EDTA/GO/Li hydrogel as an electrolyte and a carbon cloth containing activated carbon as an electrode. The supercapacitor showed stable CV curves at different bending angles, indicating tight adhesion between the hydrogel electrolyte and the electrode at a scanning rate of 500 mV/s. At a current density of 0.5 A/g, the mass specific capacitances of the supercapacitors at −20 °C, 25 °C, and 80 °C were 41.2 F/g, 52.3 F/g, and 65.4 F/g, respectively. The decrease in capacitance at low temperatures ascribed to the partial condensation of water, which can lead to the blockage of ion conduction. The increase in capacitance at high temperatures is mainly due to the rapid diffusion of ions.

In recent years, machine learning, as a research area of artificial intelligence, has been widely used in data analysis and pattern recognition. Human-computer interaction is a crucial aspect of machine learning and the conversion between electrical and digital signals enables us to clearly observe changes in signals in programming software and facilitates the processing of data. Hydrogels, as a sensing and interface material, is well-suited for the human-computer interaction process due to its compressibility and good stability, making it easier to develop in human-computer interaction applications.

The combination of the highly electrically conductive CNTs and graphene and the adhesive hydrogels makes the composite hydrogels well suitable for displaying the transmission of signals in human-computer interaction. The resistance changes recorded by the hydrogels prepared by Deng et al. (Deng et al., 2019), can be wirelessly transmitted to a smartphone via Bluetooth or the Internet for easy remote monitoring (Fig. 8 (a)). Feng et al. (Feng et al., 2022) prepared a multi-crosslinked PVA/EG/CNT-OH click-organic hydrogel using photoinitiated thiol-ene click chemistry. The hydrogel has the advantages of fast response, stable sensing, fast self-recovery performance, small hysteresis, and remarkable response to small strain and pressure fluctuation. By preparing the hydrogel into a writing board and writing letters randomly, then importing the signal into the computer, the same 14 features of the signal are extracted, and finally the MATLAB program is used to calculate the input signal features thus performing the recognition of letters (Fig. 8(b)). Wang et al. (Z. Wang et al., 2022) designed a graphene conductive hydrogel as a smart insole and constructed a gait recognition model based on algorithms that can be used for human daily behavioral gait recognition and human pathological gait recognition. Four strain sensors based on 0.02 wt% GO-PAM hydrogel was installed on the soles and heels of the left and right human feet, respectively. The analog voltage signals generated by the four strain sensors were collected. Next, the collected analog voltage signals are A/D converted and transferred from the microcontroller to the PC. Then, the collected original signals are filtered by fast Fourier transform (FFT) to remove the noise and retain the useful features of the original signals. Finally, neural network model, decision tree model and random forest model are constructed to identify and analyze the gait data, respectively (Fig. 8(c)). Xie et al. (Xie et al., 2022) had made graphene conductive hydrogel to produce smart gloves that can monitor hand motion in real time. The wearable flexible sensor monitors human activity wirelessly via a Bluetooth connection, and the data is synchronized to a cell phone for display. The hydrogels were also attached to each finger of the glove separately, and the response of each finger was independent and distinguishable, facilitating gesture recognition. In the results, the resistance was found to exhibit a stable and repeatable response (Fig. 8(d)).

Hydrogel is a 3D network structure with adhesion, stretchability, and biocompatibility. In the field of biomedicine, these advantages make hydrogel useful for wound dressing, antibacterial applications, and hemostasis. However, for photothermal therapy applications, hydrogels need to exhibit temperature changes under illumination to achieve sterilization and hemostasis. Carbon nanoparticles are materials with excellent photothermal properties due to their sp3, sp2 hybrid, and π electron clouds, which have similar energy levels and wide absorption spectra for visible light. When photons are absorbed by carbon nanoparticles, they release energy, leading to temperature changes. Therefore, the combination of carbon nanoparticles and hydrogels can enhance the photothermal treatment performance of hydrogels.

Chao et al. (Chao et al., 2023) developed a hydrogel by adding biomass lignin and MWCNTs to a polyvinyl alcohol matrix, resulting in a hydrogel with photothermal antibacterial properties. The MWCNTs in the hydrogel exhibited excellent photothermal properties, as they can absorb a wide range of electromagnetic waves under near-infrared (NIR) irradiation at 808 nm and 1.5 W/cm2. The photothermal properties of the hydrogel were significantly enhanced with increasing MWCNTs content. The wound healing process was then monitored at different times, and on the 7th day, all wounds exhibited significant contraction. On the 14th day, the MWCNTs + NIR experimental group showed the best healing effect, with a wound shrinkage rate of 95.28% (Fig. 9 (a)). Li et al. (Li et al., 2023) synthesized magneto-electric nanosheets (MPG) containing PDA on the surface of rGO using an in-situ method. The MPG nanosheets were introduced into the hydrogel network under the influence of a magnetic field. The hydrogels exhibited good photothermal conversion performance due to the excellent absorption properties of rGO in the near-infrared region. Under NIR irradiation at 808 nm, the temperature of the hydrogel without MPG nanosheets did not change much, whereas the temperature of the hydrogel with 1 wt% MPG nanosheets increased from 16 °C to 45 °C. Moreover, the photothermal conversion performance of the hydrogel increased with increasing nanosheet content. The antibacterial effects of the coated plate method and co-culture method on Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were compared, and it was found that the antibacterial effect improved with increasing MPG nanosheets content (Fig. 9(b)).

Yue et al. (Yue et al., 2022) prepared CD@Hydrogel by carrying out a Schiff base reaction using amino-rich CDs. When exposed to near-infrared irradiation at 660 nm, the hydrogels exhibited a significant anti-tumor effect. After 5 min of laser irradiation, the temperature at the tumor site increased significantly to 48.6 °C. The results of H&E (hematoxylin and eosin) and TUNEL (TdT-mediated dUTP Nick-End Labeling) staining demonstrated that the CD@Hydrogel group had the highest apoptosis rate (Fig. 9(c)). Dai et al. (Dai et al., 2020) prepared DOX@PNCFs by dispersing porous carbon nanofibers (PNCFs) and the anti-tumor drug doxorubicin (DOX) in a hydrogel, resulting in hydrogels with excellent near-infrared absorption and high photothermal conversion ability. The porous structure of the hydrogel allows for high drug absorption and a shortened drug release rate. The test of photothermal ablation ability showed that the tumor site of mice injected with DOX@PCNFs hydrogel heated rapidly under near-infrared irradiation, demonstrating the photothermal conversion ability of PCNFs. Moreover, histological investigations of tumors treated with different methods revealed that the DOX@PCNFs + NIR group was more effective in inhibiting tumor growth. Two weeks later, H&E staining of major organs showed no significant damage or negative effects (Fig. 9(d)).

The development of CNPCH has been rapid and hydrogels with good mechanical properties, electrical conductivity, and tensile strength have also demonstrated their sensitivity, self-healing abilities, etc. in various applications. This review outlines the role of carbon nanoparticles in the overall hydrogel network, analyzing the impact of carbon nanoparticles on hydrogels, and finally, highlights some applications of CNPCH.

Currently, conductive hydrogels based on carbon nanoparticles have been studied extensively and have demonstrated potential applications in wearable electronic devices and other fields. However, these studies were carried out under experimental conditions and there is still much work to do and to apply theory into practice. Present challenges toward practical applications of CNPCH and roles of carbon nanoparticles are discussed in the following six parts, ranging from the mechanical property of hydrogels, comparison of different carbon nanomaterials, biological toxicity, microstructure tailoring, to the multifunction design and future manufacturing techniques.
(1)
Mechanical properties. Since the moduli of hydrogels are similar to human soft tissues, stress-strain behavior of hydrogels plays a significant role in biological tissue engineering. However, from the perspective of daily human behavior, the wear and fatigue of human soft tissues is a big issue. For example, the knee joint of an ordinary person needs to endure the pressure cycle of 4–9 MPa for millions of times per year, and the fracture energy after the cycle must be at least 1000 J/m2. Hence, the anti-fatigue threshold of hydrogel can only meet the basic requirement of a replacement soft tissue by reaching 1000 J/m2 (Creton, 2017; Taylor et al., 2012). In this regard, fatigue resistance is a great challenge for hydrogels, so how to exploit the mechanical properties of carbon nanoparticles to improve the fatigue threshold of hydrogels is a very important research direction. In addition, the flexibility of hydrogels makes them highly ductile, but it also decreases their impact resistance simultaneously. In daily life, stress-strain sensors will inevitably face various shocks, so it is necessary for the hydrogels to maintain high impact resistance and functionality in harsh environments. Carbon nanoparticles are relatively stiff and elastic, thus having potential for use in impact-resistant hydrogels by innovative structure design and development of high-performance composites
(2)
Comparison of different carbon nanomaterials. In addition to CNTs mainly discussed here, there are also other typical carbon nanomaterials such as graphene, CDs, and CNFs. They provide a rich pool of materials with different dimensions, widely tailored structures and surface characteristics, enabling construction of various high-performance composites with broadly extended applications areas. For example, 2D GO sheets or 0D CDs could be introduced into hydrogels to tune the mechanical, electrical and optical properties; combination of flexible hydrogels with fluorescent CDs (at particular wavelengths ranging from visible to near infrared) could enable implanted or wearable electronics for bio-detection, diagnosis and medical care. These 2D sheets or quantum dots generally could be dispersed easily within the hydrogel by solution processing, without much entanglement like 1D CNTs; in comparison, CNTs and CNFs are more suitable candidates to form percolated conductive network throughout the polymer matrix to improve the electrical conductivity and thermal management of hydrogel-based devices. Also, GO sheets and CDs could be synthesized from natural precursors such as exfoliation of expanded graphite or annealing of biomass, giving the promise of large-scale, low-cost industrial production which is critical for future commercialization.
(3)
Bio-toxicity. Carbon nanoparticles have been widely used in daily life, therefore could produce a significant environmental impact. Therefore, the toxicity and side effects of carbon nanoparticles have drawn significant attention from industry, public and governments. The issues of neurotoxicity, hepatotoxicity, and renal toxicity associated with carbon nanoparticles indicate their potential harm to human health, and will be more complicated considering different dimensions and structure characteristics of CNTs, graphene and CDs. Although various methods have been proposed to address the biological toxicity of carbon nanoparticles, such as attaching PDA and proteins on their surface, more effective and straightforward approaches are still needed to mitigate the biological toxicity of carbon nanoparticles. Future study on the bio-compatible hydrogel functional materials/devices is imperative toward practical applications.
(4)
Structure tailoring. When carbon nanoparticles are used as conductive fillers, the self-aggregation problem of these nanoscale materials is also a crucial issue. Despite the pore-rich network structure of hydrogels providing space for the uniform-intercalation of carbon nanoparticles, the stretching and conductivity of hydrogels can still sharply decrease when a certain amount of carbon nanoparticles are added. In this respect, improving the addition/dispersion of carbon fillers and composite fabrication methods is still necessary. Furthermore, recently reported 3D carbon nanomaterial structures such as CNT and graphene aerogels/foams can be used as porous templates to directly fill in polymers, solving the problem of nanofiller aggregation and achieving uniform distribution of conductive carbon nanoparticles networks throughout the polymer matrix (Lin et al., 2016; Y. Wu, Zhao, et al., 2021). It is promising to utilize these novel fabrication techniques to develop high performance functional composite hydrogels for energy and environmental applications.
(5)
Multifunctional design. Integrated multifunctional systems based on hydrogels combining unique mechanical, electrical and thermal properties deserve further in-depth investigation. For example, by synergistically combining the highly conductive carbon nanomaterial networks with the ultra-flexible and stretchable properties of the hydrogel, we can design and fabricate various types of sensors (responding to stress/strain, temperature, moisture, etc.), 3D flexible and porous electrodes, and thermally or electrically driven actuators, and explore their potential applications in areas such as flexible/wearable functional devices, artificial muscles, and next-generation energy storage systems.
(6)
Advanced manufacturing techniques. Emerging material fabrication techniques such as 4D printing could be a future direction to enable rational design and low-cost fabrication of novel structure, functional hydrogels, which can exhibit controlled shape/structure change in response to external or environmental stimuli such as illumination, magnetic fields, pH levels, temperature, and humidity. Carbon nanoparticles could be blended with organic molecules to make homogeneous inks with suitable viscosity for smooth printing, followed by subsequent drying or solvent treatment to form predefined porous aerogels or hydrogels. The incorporation of carbon nanoparticle-based networks within the hydrogel could not only reinforce the structure, but also promote optical absorption, interfacial heat transfer, and enable the change in electrical conductivity upon exposure to different environmental conditions. Additionally, the reversible deformation of 4D printed hydrogels is primarily caused by differences in swelling degree due to stress mismatch, which can be manipulated by the physical and chemical properties of incorporated carbon nanoparticles. Thus, the resulting 4D composite hydrogels have the potential to substantially enhance the performance as sensors and actuators, such as the response time, sensitivity, output stress/strain during actuation, which are important criteria for applications in the field of soft robotics and wearable electronics.

In summary, there are many prospects for the future of CNPCH, with wide applications in various industries such as aerospace, electromagnetic shielding, and automobile. It is believed that with further experimental research and advancements, industrial-level hydrogels will establish their own niche in daily life and bring convenience to various aspects of our lives.
